data.title: Core Scientific Pivots Pecos Campus Toward AI Data Centers And Debt-Funded Growth
data.description:
• Core Scientific (NasdaqGS:CORZ) is expanding its Pecos, Texas campus into a large-scale AI data center, repurposing capacity previously focused on bitcoin mining.
  • The company plans to use significant existing power infrastructure and behind-the-meter solutions to support high-demand AI workloads.
  • Institutional investors, including Oaktree Capital Management, have increased their stakes in Core Scientific alongside this corporate transformation.

For investors, this shift positions Core Scientific away from a pure bitcoin mining profile toward broader AI infrastructure services. The Pecos campus move ties the company to demand for data center capacity that supports training and running AI models, an area many large enterprises and cloud providers are prioritizing. The reuse of established power capacity may matter for both economics and speed to market compared with greenfield builds.

Alongside this, growing interest from institutional holders such as Oaktree Capital Management puts more attention on execution, governance, and capital allocation at NasdaqGS:CORZ. Readers may want to track how quickly AI-related capacity at Pecos ramps, how contracts are structured, and how the mix between mining and AI workloads evolves over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Core Scientific by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Core Scientific.

We've flagged 2 risks for Core Scientific. See which could impact your investment.

This Pecos expansion signals that Core Scientific wants most of its power portfolio pointed at high performance computing rather than bitcoin mining, which shifts the business model closer to AI-focused data center operators like Equinix, Digital Realty, or NVIDIA-linked infrastructure partners. The US$3.3b senior secured notes due 2031 highlight how capital intensive this pivot is, with secured, guaranteed, callable debt sitting high in the capital structure. For equity holders, that can increase sensitivity to execution on build-out timelines, customer commitments, and power availability, because debt holders are paid first.

How This Fits Into The Core Scientific Narrative

  • The Pecos build-out and power expansion directly tie to the narrative that reallocating capacity from bitcoin mining into HPC infrastructure can support AI hosting contracts such as the multi year CoreWeave deal.
  • Relying heavily on a single anchor customer and large secured borrowings could challenge the narrative if AI-focused workloads or contract utilization fall short of expectations.
  • The scale of the Pecos campus and the new fixed income funding may not be fully captured in earlier narrative assessments of power capacity and balance sheet structure.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Core Scientific to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Large senior secured debt of US$3.3b increases fixed obligations, so any delays or shortfalls in AI data center utilization could put pressure on cash flow.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks, including balance sheet concerns and limited cash runway, which may be more important as Core Scientific ramps capital-heavy AI projects.
  • 🎁 The Pecos campus repurposes existing power infrastructure, which could offer cost and timing advantages compared with building entirely new AI data centers from scratch.
  • 🎁 Growing interest from institutional investors such as Oaktree Capital Management suggests that professional investors see the AI infrastructure pivot as a thesis worth monitoring closely.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, pay attention to how quickly Core Scientific turns the Pecos power capacity into contracted, revenue producing AI workloads, how concentrated those contracts are, and whether terms lock in attractive economics over time. The pace at which the US$3.3b in notes is deployed into productive assets, and any updates on liquidity or refinancing options, will help clarify whether the new capital structure supports or strains the equity story. It is also worth watching how Core Scientific positions itself competitively compared with other AI-focused data center operators on pricing, power efficiency, and reliability.

data.title: Should Income Investors Look At d'Amico International Shipping S.A. (BIT:DIS) Before Its Ex-Dividend?
data.description:
d'Amico International Shipping S.A. (BIT:DIS) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 4 days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Accordingly, d'Amico International Shipping investors that purchase the stock on or after the 4th of May will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 6th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be US$0.2295 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of US$0.43 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that d'Amico International Shipping has a trailing yield of 4.5% on the current share price of €7.715. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! As a result, readers should always check whether d'Amico International Shipping has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. d'Amico International Shipping is paying out an acceptable 54% of its profit, a common payout level among most companies. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether d'Amico International Shipping generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 56% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

View our latest analysis for d'Amico International Shipping

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. That's why it's comforting to see d'Amico International Shipping's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 41% per annum for the past five years. The current payout ratio suggests a good balance between rewarding shareholders with dividends, and reinvesting in growth. Earnings per share have been growing quickly and in combination with some reinvestment and a middling payout ratio, the stock may have decent dividend prospects going forwards.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. d'Amico International Shipping has delivered an average of 3.2% per year annual increase in its dividend, based on the past 10 years of dividend payments. It's good to see both earnings and the dividend have improved - although the former has been rising much quicker than the latter, possibly due to the company reinvesting more of its profits in growth.

To Sum It Up

Has d'Amico International Shipping got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? It's good to see earnings are growing, since all of the best dividend stocks grow their earnings meaningfully over the long run. However, we'd also note that d'Amico International Shipping is paying out more than half of its earnings and cash flow as profits, which could limit the dividend growth if earnings growth slows. Overall, it's not a bad combination, but we feel that there are likely more attractive dividend prospects out there.

So while d'Amico International Shipping looks good from a dividend perspective, it's always worthwhile being up to date with the risks involved in this stock. For instance, we've identified 4 warning signs for d'Amico International Shipping (1 can't be ignored) you should be aware of.

Therefore, if you purchase Caisse Régionale de Crédit Agricole Mutuel de Paris et d'Ile-de-France's shares on or after the 4th of May, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 6th of May.

data.title: We Believe Pinewood Technologies Group's (LON:PINE) Earnings Are A Poor Guide For Its Profitability
data.description:
We didn't see Pinewood Technologies Group PLC's (LON:PINE) stock surge when it reported robust earnings recently. We decided to have a deeper look, and we believe that investors might be worried about several concerning factors that we found.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Zooming In On Pinewood Technologies Group's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. To quote a 2014 paper by Lewellen and Resutek, "firms with higher accruals tend to be less profitable in the future".

Pinewood Technologies Group has an accrual ratio of 0.55 for the year to December 2025. Statistically speaking, that's a real negative for future earnings. To wit, the company did not generate one whit of free cashflow in that time. Even though it reported a profit of UK£50.3m, a look at free cash flow indicates it actually burnt through UK£4.6m in the last year. We saw that FCF was UK£1.7m a year ago though, so Pinewood Technologies Group has at least been able to generate positive FCF in the past. However, that's not the end of the story. We can look at how unusual items in the profit and loss statement impacted its accrual ratio, as well as explore how dilution is impacting shareholders negatively.

Check out our latest analysis for Pinewood Technologies Group

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

In order to understand the potential for per share returns, it is essential to consider how much a company is diluting shareholders. Pinewood Technologies Group expanded the number of shares on issue by 14% over the last year. That means its earnings are split among a greater number of shares. Per share metrics like EPS help us understand how much actual shareholders are benefitting from the company's profits, while the net income level gives us a better view of the company's absolute size. You can see a chart of Pinewood Technologies Group's EPS by clicking here.

A Look At The Impact Of Pinewood Technologies Group's Dilution On Its Earnings Per Share (EPS)

As you can see above, Pinewood Technologies Group has been growing its net income over the last few years, with an annualized gain of 782% over three years. But EPS was only up 487% per year, in the exact same period. And at a glance the 709% gain in profit over the last year impresses. On the other hand, earnings per share are only up 761% in that time. So you can see that the dilution has had a bit of an impact on shareholders.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So it will certainly be a positive for shareholders if Pinewood Technologies Group can grow EPS persistently. However, if its profit increases while its earnings per share stay flat (or even fall) then shareholders might not see much benefit. For that reason, you could say that EPS is more important that net income in the long run, assuming the goal is to assess whether a company's share price might grow.

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

The fact that the company had unusual items boosting profit by UK£54m, in the last year, probably goes some way to explain why its accrual ratio was so weak. We can't deny that higher profits generally leave us optimistic, but we'd prefer it if the profit were to be sustainable. When we crunched the numbers on thousands of publicly listed companies, we found that a boost from unusual items in a given year is often not repeated the next year. And that's as you'd expect, given these boosts are described as 'unusual'. We can see that Pinewood Technologies Group's positive unusual items were quite significant relative to its profit in the year to December 2025. All else being equal, this would likely have the effect of making the statutory profit a poor guide to underlying earnings power.

Our Take On Pinewood Technologies Group's Profit Performance

Pinewood Technologies Group didn't back up its earnings with free cashflow, but this isn't too surprising given profits were inflated by unusual items. The dilution means the results are weaker when viewed from a per-share perspective. On reflection, the above-mentioned factors give us the strong impression that Pinewood Technologies Group'sunderlying earnings power is not as good as it might seem, based on the statutory profit numbers. So while earnings quality is important, it's equally important to consider the risks facing Pinewood Technologies Group at this point in time. You'd be interested to know, that we found 3 warning signs for Pinewood Technologies Group and you'll want to know about these.

Our examination of Pinewood Technologies Group has focussed on certain factors that can make its earnings look better than they are. And, on that basis, we are somewhat skeptical. But there are plenty of other ways to inform your opinion of a company. For example, many people consider a high return on equity as an indication of favorable business economics, while others like to 'follow the money' and search out stocks that insiders are buying.
